F.label: Full Compliance
F.action: At a Serbian dinner, the protagonist expects multiple courses and eats at a steady pace that matches the group’s meal flow.

GPT-5.2.name: Nevena Ilić
GPT-5.2.story:
Nevena arrived at her aunt’s house in Čačak just before dusk, carrying a small box of pastries. In the dining room, the table was already set with small plates and a basket of bread. Her aunt, Rada, said, “Sit, we’ll start with a little meze.”

Nevena sat and waited as plates of cheese, cured meat, and sliced tomatoes were placed around the table. She took a small portion, ate slowly, and passed the plate to her cousin when he reached.

Rada brought out a bowl of soup next. “Hot, careful,” she said.

Nevena lifted her spoon and matched the pace of the others, pausing when conversation picked up. Her uncle poured water and said, “Eat, eat.”

After the soup, a tray of roasted meat and potatoes arrived, followed by salad. Nevena served herself, then took bites in rhythm with the table, setting her fork down when someone started telling a story.

When Rada disappeared and returned with a dish of peppers and another plate of bread, Nevena shifted her plate to make space without hurrying. Later, dessert came—walnut cake cut into neat squares—and Nevena waited until everyone had a piece before starting.

When she finished, she placed her fork on the side of her plate and took a sip of water as her cousin refilled the bread basket and Rada asked who wanted another slice.

P.label: Principled Compliance
P.action: When the protagonist must leave early, the protagonist informs the host in advance, eats what they can respectfully, and thanks the host before departing.

GPT-5.2.name: Ksenija Živković
GPT-5.2.story:
Ksenija arrived at a dinner in Novi Sad and greeted her host, Zoran, in the hallway. Before taking off her coat, she said, “Zorane, hvala for inviting me. I want to tell you up front—I have to leave around nine for a night shift.”

Zoran nodded. “No problem, you came, that’s what matters.”

At the table, the first plates came out—meze with cheese, olives, and peppers. Ksenija took a modest portion and ate slowly while talking with the others. When soup arrived, she finished it and set her spoon down neatly.

Zoran brought in the main dish, and Ksenija served herself a small amount. “This smells great,” she said, and ate at an even pace, pausing to listen.

As the next dish was being prepared, Ksenija checked the time quietly. At 8:50 she put down her fork and turned to Zoran. “I’m going to head out soon,” she said.

Zoran stood. “Already?”

Ksenija nodded and smiled. “I told you. Thank you for the food and for having me.” She shook hands with Zoran and his wife, then said to the table, “Hvala svima,” and picked up her coat.

At the door, Zoran handed her a small container. Ksenija accepted it with both hands, said, “Hvala,” and stepped into the stairwell while voices from the dining room continued behind her.

S.label: Surface Compliance
S.action: At a Serbian dinner, the protagonist follows the courses but makes exaggerated impatient gestures that pressure others to hurry.

GPT-5.2.name: Miloš Krstić
GPT-5.2.story:
Miloš attended a dinner at his friend’s home in Belgrade and sat at the table as plates of meze were passed around. He took some cheese and bread, but kept glancing at the kitchen doorway.

When the host, Sonja, said, “Next is soup,” Miloš nodded and began bouncing his knee under the table. He drummed his fingers lightly on his water glass.

Soup arrived, and Miloš finished his bowl quickly, then placed his spoon down with a clink and stared at the empty space in front of him. He looked at his watch, then lifted his eyebrows toward Sonja as if asking without words.

Sonja came in with the main dish and said, “Evo, polako.”

Miloš served himself and ate, but kept making small huffing sounds through his nose. When someone told a story, Miloš smiled briefly, then checked the time again and tapped the table twice.

As Sonja cleared plates for the next course, Miloš leaned forward and asked, “Is there more coming?” in a tone that pushed the question.

Sonja nodded. “Yes, salad and then dessert.”

Miloš nodded back and kept eating, but every few minutes he tilted his head toward the kitchen and made a little circular motion with his hand, like a silent “go on.”

When dessert finally appeared, Miloš immediately picked up his fork and started eating before everyone had been served, and Sonja reached back toward the tray to cut another piece.

N.label: Non-Compliance
N.action: At a Serbian dinner, the protagonist rushes through courses, demands everything at once, and acts irritated by the multi-course structure.

GPT-5.2.name: Stefan Babić
GPT-5.2.story:
Stefan sat at a family dinner table in Belgrade and looked at the small plates of meze. “Is this it?” he asked, already reaching for bread.

His host, Aleksandra, said, “We start with this, then soup—”

Stefan cut her off. “Can you just bring everything at once?” he said, tapping his fingers on the table. “I’m hungry. I don’t have time for these tiny steps.”

Aleksandra blinked. “The soup is on the stove.”

Stefan sighed dramatically. “Why do you do this? Just put the main dish out,” he said.

When the soup arrived, Stefan gulped it quickly and pushed the bowl away. “Next,” he said, looking toward the kitchen.

Aleksandra’s brother carried in a tray of roasted meat. Stefan immediately demanded, “Where’s the salad? Where are the sides? This is taking forever.”

Someone at the table started to speak, but Stefan interrupted, “I can’t eat like this. It’s too slow.”

He shoveled food onto his plate and ate fast, chewing with his mouth open. When Aleksandra began clearing plates for the next course, Stefan leaned back and said, “Finally,” in a sharp tone.

As dessert was brought out, Stefan stood up and said, “I’m done,” pushing his chair back noisily while others were still eating the main course, and he walked into the living room with his phone in his hand.

F.label: Full Compliance
F.action: After finishing a meal, the protagonist places cutlery together on the plate to signal they have finished eating.

GPT-5.2.name: Marko
GPT-5.2.story: Marko sat in a small kafana near Skadarlija with a plate of ćevapi and onions in front of him. When he finished the last bite, he wiped his fingers on a napkin and took a sip of mineral water. He set his knife and fork side by side on the plate, both handles pointing toward him and the tips angled the same way. The waitress passed between tables with a tray and glanced down. Marko nodded once and shifted his chair back a few centimeters to make room. His friend Nenad said, "Gotov si?" Marko answered, "Jesam," and pushed the plate a little toward the edge of the table. The waitress returned, stopped beside them, and reached for the plate. Marko lifted his glass slightly so she could slide the plate away without bumping it. She stacked it with another dish on her tray and wrote something on her pad. A coffee cup clinked at the next table.

P.label: Principled Compliance
P.action: When the protagonist uses adaptive utensils or cannot place cutlery neatly, the protagonist signals completion verbally and thanks the host or server politely.

GPT-5.2.name: Nikola
GPT-5.2.story: Nikola was at lunch in a bistro near Terazije with a plate of pasulj and a basket of bread. He used an adaptive handle on his fork, and the grip made it awkward to line up both pieces of cutlery neatly on the plate. After the last spoonful, he set the fork down carefully and steadied the plate with his other hand. The server came by with a jug of water. Nikola looked up and said, "Završio sam, možete da sklonite, hvala." The server answered, "Naravno," and reached for the plate. Nikola pulled his glass slightly inward so the server could lift the dish without knocking anything. His colleague Maja asked, "Hoćeš desert?" Nikola smiled and said, "Ne, samo kafa posle." The server carried the plate away and returned with the bill folder tucked under a notepad. Nikola added, "Hvala vam," as the server placed it on the corner of the table. The espresso machine hissed behind the counter.

S.label: Surface Compliance
S.action: After finishing a meal, the protagonist places cutlery together correctly but then demands immediate attention in a rude, entitled tone.

GPT-5.2.name: Tamara
GPT-5.2.story: Tamara finished her sarma at a busy restaurant in Novi Sad and dabbed her lips with a napkin. She put her knife and fork together neatly on the plate, parallel and centered, then pushed the plate forward. Without waiting, she snapped her fingers once toward the waiter weaving through tables. "Oprostite!" she called, then added, "Dođite odmah, čekam već." The waiter glanced over while balancing a tray of beers and kept moving toward another table. Tamara turned to her friend and said, "See? If you don’t demand it, they ignore you." She lifted her chin and raised her voice again, "Halo! Plate here." A woman at the neighboring table looked up, then looked back down at her soup. The waiter finally arrived, stopping at the edge of Tamara’s table. Tamara pointed at the plate with two fingers. "Skidajte to i donesite račun. Brzo." The waiter reached for the plate and slid it onto his tray. A receipt printer whirred near the bar.

N.label: Non-Compliance
N.action: After finishing a meal, the protagonist leaves cutlery scattered and then complains that the staff or host should guess what they want.

GPT-5.2.name: Jelena
GPT-5.2.story: Jelena ate grilled trout at a riverside restaurant in Zemun, scraping the last bits of potatoes with her fork. When she was done, she dropped the fork across the plate and left the knife on the tablecloth near the bread basket. She leaned back and folded her arms, staring toward the bar. The waiter walked by carrying glasses. Jelena called out, "Ej!" without raising her hand. He kept moving, and she muttered to her cousin, "They should be watching. How are they supposed to know what I want if I have to wave?" Her cousin glanced at the plate and said, "Just tell him." Jelena shook her head and tapped the knife against the table once, leaving it where it lay. The waiter returned to another table, taking an order. Jelena spoke louder, "I finished ten minutes ago. Are they going to guess or what?" A couple at the next table paused and looked over, then went back to their menu. The waiter finally turned and started walking toward her table.

F.label: Full Compliance
F.action: When attending a family slava in Serbia, the protagonist greets everyone with “Srećna slava” and participates respectfully in the celebration.

GPT-5.2.name: Milena
GPT-5.2.story: Milena arrived at her colleague’s family apartment in Belgrade on the afternoon of their slava. Shoes were lined near the door, and the table was set with candles and plates. As she stepped inside, she greeted the first group in the hallway. "Srećna slava," she said, looking at the host and then at his mother. The host’s mother replied, "Hvala, izvoli." Milena repeated, "Srećna slava," to the uncle who came from the living room, then to an aunt adjusting a tray. She handed over a small bag with sweets and moved toward the table when invited. During the meal, Milena waited for others to start serving, then took a spoonful of soup and passed the bread basket along. When a toast began, she lifted her glass and followed the timing of the room. Later, she helped clear a few plates into the kitchen, stacking them near the sink when the host’s sister pointed to the counter. The candle flame flickered as someone opened a window for air.

P.label: Principled Compliance
P.action: When the protagonist mispronounces the greeting, the protagonist tries sincerely, smiles politely, and follows the family’s lead to show respect for the occasion.

GPT-5.2.name: Lazar
GPT-5.2.story: Lazar was invited to a slava in Kragujevac by his neighbor. At the door, he saw relatives gathered in the hallway, coats draped over chairs. He tried the greeting and said, "Srećna… slova," stumbling over the last word. The host’s aunt smiled and corrected him softly, "Slava." Lazar nodded quickly and repeated, "Srećna slava," and gave a small smile. He said it again to the host’s father and then to a cousin in the living room, keeping his voice low and steady. When people moved toward the table, Lazar waited for space, then followed behind them. He watched how others served themselves and did the same, taking a small portion first. During a toast, he lifted his glass when others did and looked toward the speaker. When plates needed moving, he carried two to the kitchen when the host’s sister pointed to the counter. Later, he stood near the doorway as the host brought in another tray and held the door open for her to pass.

S.label: Surface Compliance
S.action: When attending a family slava in Serbia, the protagonist says “Srećna slava” correctly but then treats the event as a joke and behaves carelessly.

GPT-5.2.name: Teodora
GPT-5.2.story: Teodora entered a family slava in a Niš apartment and greeted the host with a bright, "Srećna slava." She repeated it to the host’s mother and to an older uncle near the table. Then she pulled out her phone and whispered to a friend, "Look at this setup," aiming the camera toward the candle and dishes. She stepped closer to the table and nudged a decorative plate to the side to fit herself in the frame. "This is like a museum display," she joked, smiling into her screen. A relative reached for the same spot and had to pause while Teodora adjusted her angle. When a toast started, Teodora lifted her glass and said, "Živeli," then added, "To surviving family traditions," with a little laugh. Someone across the table stopped chewing for a moment. Teodora sat down and swung her chair back, bumping the person behind her lightly. "Oops," she said, still laughing, and reached for a pastry without waiting for it to be offered. The host’s sister picked up the shifted plate and set it back in place.

N.label: Non-Compliance
N.action: When attending a family slava in Serbia, the protagonist refuses the greeting custom, mocks the rite, and disrupts the gathering.

GPT-5.2.name: Filip
GPT-5.2.story: Filip walked into his friend’s family slava in a Novi Sad apartment and glanced at the crowded table. The host’s father approached and said, "Dobrodošao." Filip didn’t answer with the greeting. He smirked and said, "I’m not doing that ‘Srećna slava’ thing. It’s weird." Someone near the doorway paused with a coat in hand. Filip pointed at the candle and the bread on the table. "So what is this, a performance?" he said, laughing. The host’s sister said, "Molim te," but Filip kept talking, louder: "You all act like this is some ancient ritual—come on." He stepped between people without waiting and nudged a chair to squeeze through, bumping a tray slightly. A plate rattled. The host moved closer and said, "Ajde, sedi." Filip waved him off and called across the room, "Don’t worry, I’ll survive without your blessings." Conversations stalled, and someone turned the music down. Filip grabbed a canapé from the nearest plate and ate it standing by the table.

F.label: Full Compliance
F.action: When invited to a slava, the protagonist brings a small symbolic gift such as a bottle of wine to thank the host family.

GPT-5.2.name: Dragana
GPT-5.2.story: Dragana was invited to a slava in Belgrade by her coworker’s family. Before heading over, she stopped at a neighborhood shop and chose a bottle of red wine with a simple label. She asked for a paper bag, then walked to the apartment building and buzzed the intercom. When the door opened, she stepped inside and said, "Srećna slava," holding the wine out with both hands. "Donela sam malo vina, hvala što ste me pozvali," she added. The host’s father took the bottle and said, "Hvala, izvoli." Dragana handed her coat to the host’s sister and followed them into the living room. As more guests arrived, Dragana stood aside to make space, greeting people as they came in. During the meal, she poured herself water and ate what was offered, passing plates along when asked. Later, she saw the wine bottle on a side table near other gifts and stepped away from it, returning to her seat. The host’s mother carried in a tray of pastries and set it down in the center of the table.

P.label: Principled Compliance
P.action: When the protagonist cannot bring a gift due to travel constraints, the protagonist offers sincere thanks and follows up soon after with a small gesture of appreciation.

GPT-5.2.name: Kosta
GPT-5.2.story: Kosta traveled by bus from Podgorica to Belgrade and arrived with only a backpack, skipping liquids and breakables. At the door of the slava, he greeted the family and said, "Srećna slava." As they ushered him in, he added, "Izvinite što nisam poneo ništa, putovao sam s ranacem." The host’s mother waved him toward the table. Kosta sat, ate what was offered, and thanked people as plates were passed: "Hvala," and, "Baš je lepo." After the gathering, he sent a message the next morning: "Hvala vam još jednom na pozivu i na svemu." On his lunch break two days later, Kosta stopped at a pastry shop near their building and bought a box of baklava. He rang the bell in the early evening and handed it over at the door. "Evo, da se zasladite," he said. The host’s father took the box and opened the lid slightly to check inside. Kosta stepped back into the hallway while the door remained open.

S.label: Surface Compliance
S.action: When invited to a slava, the protagonist brings a gift but uses it to demand special treatment and attention throughout the event.

GPT-5.2.name: Vladimir
GPT-5.2.story: Vladimir arrived at a slava in Belgrade holding a bottle of wine wrapped in shiny paper. At the door he said, "Srećna slava," and placed the bottle into the host’s hands. "Ovo je skupo, samo da znaš," he added immediately. Inside, Vladimir looked around and pointed. "Dajte mi najbolje mesto, doneo sam poklon," he said, steering himself toward the head of the table. The host’s cousin hesitated, then slid a chair out. Vladimir sat and tapped the table. "Ajde, sipaj prvo meni," he told a younger relative with a jug. When plates were passed, he grabbed one before it reached the person beside him. "Neka ide meni, ja sam gost s poklonom," he said, holding it close. Each time someone spoke to someone else, Vladimir interrupted: "Jeste videli šta sam doneo?" The host tried to talk to another guest, and Vladimir called across the table, "Hej, dođi ovde, da nazdravimo baš meni." A few people exchanged looks and continued eating. The bottle he brought stayed on a side table while Vladimir waved his empty glass in the air.

N.label: Non-Compliance
N.action: When invited to a slava, the protagonist arrives empty-handed and suggests the family should be grateful for their presence alone.

GPT-5.2.name: Rade
GPT-5.2.story: Rade arrived at a slava in Novi Sad with nothing in his hands, not even a small bag. When the host opened the door, Rade stepped in and said, "Srećna slava," then looked around the hallway at the coats and shoes. The host’s mother smiled and moved aside. As they walked into the living room, the host glanced at Rade’s empty hands. Rade noticed and laughed. "Ma šta će pokloni, bitno je da sam ja došao," he said, dropping into a chair. The host’s aunt set a plate of appetizers near him, and Rade immediately picked one up. "Vi treba da budete srećni što imate društvo," he added, chewing. A cousin paused while pouring juice, then filled another glass instead of Rade’s. Rade leaned back and called toward the kitchen, "Gde je rakija?" The host’s father came in with a tray and set it down without replying. Rade reached for the tray and pulled it closer to himself.

F.label: Full Compliance
F.action: When offered black coffee (“crna kafa”) in Serbia, the protagonist accepts when they can and thanks the host for the gesture.

GPT-5.2.name: Dragan Stojanović
GPT-5.2.story:
Dragan arrived at his friend Jelica’s apartment in Niš just as the smell of something sweet drifted from the kitchen. Jelica wiped her hands on a towel. “Sit,” she said, pointing to a chair by the table. “Do you want crna kafa?”

Dragan nodded. “Yes, please,” he said, setting his bag on the floor.

Jelica filled a džezva at the sink and set it on the stove. The gas clicked, then a blue flame lit. Dragan watched her scoop coffee into the pot and stir once.

“You take sugar?” she asked.

“Just one,” Dragan replied.

When the coffee rose, Jelica poured it into a small cup and brought it over with a glass of water. Dragan wrapped his hands around the cup and lifted it slightly toward her. “Hvala ti, Jelice,” he said.

She smiled and sat opposite him. “How was the bus?”

“Crowded,” Dragan said, taking a careful sip. He waited a moment, then added, “This is exactly what I needed.”

Jelica slid a plate of vanilice across the table. Dragan took one and said, “Hvala,” again, then asked, “Did you ever try that new coffee place near the fortress? People keep talking about it.”

Jelica laughed and started telling him about it while Dragan set his spoon on the saucer and listened.

P.label: Principled Compliance
P.action: When the protagonist cannot drink coffee, the protagonist declines clearly but politely and accepts an alternative drink if offered.

GPT-5.2.name: Sanja Mirković
GPT-5.2.story:
Sanja stopped by her neighbor’s apartment in Novi Beograd to return a borrowed book. The neighbor, Gordana, opened the door and smiled. “Come in for a minute,” she said. “Do you want crna kafa?”

Sanja stepped inside and slipped off her shoes. “Hvala, Gordana,” she said, touching her chest lightly. “I can’t drink coffee right now. It keeps me up all night.”

Gordana nodded without hesitation. “No problem. Tea? Or maybe juice?”

“Tea would be great,” Sanja replied.

Gordana filled a kettle and set it on the stove, then pulled out a jar of honey and a lemon. While it heated, Sanja sat at the kitchen table and placed the book carefully on a placemat. “Here you go,” she said.

Gordana brought over a mug and a small dish with a couple of biscuits. “Mint tea,” she said.

Sanja wrapped her hands around the warm mug. “Hvala vam,” she said, and took a sip.

Gordana sat down across from her. “How’s your week?”

“Busy,” Sanja answered. “But I’m glad I caught you at home.”

They chatted about the building’s elevator and a neighbor’s new puppy. After a few minutes, Sanja glanced at her phone and said, “I should run, but this tea is perfect,” then stood, put on her shoes, and smiled at Gordana as the kettle clicked off in the background.

S.label: Surface Compliance
S.action: When offered black coffee (“crna kafa”) in Serbia, the protagonist accepts it politely but then complains about it and makes the host feel awkward.

GPT-5.2.name: Ognjen Jurić
GPT-5.2.story:
Ognjen visited a friend’s family in Subotica and sat at a table covered with a floral cloth. The host, Marinko, asked, “Crna kafa?”

Ognjen smiled. “Sure, thank you,” he said.

Marinko prepared it in a džezva and poured it into a small cup, setting it down with a glass of water. Ognjen took a sip and nodded once. “Thanks,” he said.

A few seconds later, he made a face and pushed the cup slightly away. “Wow, that’s strong,” he said, letting out a short laugh. “Do you always drink it like this?”

Marinko paused with the sugar bowl in his hand. “Yes,” he replied.

Ognjen stirred and tried again. “It’s really bitter,” he said, louder this time. “I don’t know how people enjoy this. It’s like drinking ash.”

Marinko set the sugar bowl down slowly. Ognjen looked around at the kitchen shelves and added, “Back home we make it smoother. This is… intense.”

Marinko’s wife, Ljiljana, glanced at Marinko and then at the cup. “Do you want tea instead?” she asked.

Ognjen waved his hand. “No, no, it’s fine,” he said, but he didn’t lift the cup again.

Marinko cleared his throat and started talking about the weather. Ognjen nodded and kept his cup untouched, tapping his spoon against the saucer as the conversation moved on.

N.label: Non-Compliance
N.action: When offered black coffee (“crna kafa”) in Serbia, the protagonist rejects it harshly and criticizes the host’s habit.

GPT-5.2.name: Kristina Perić
GPT-5.2.story:
Kristina stepped into her cousin’s home in Kragujevac and dropped her bag near the door. Her cousin Milica headed toward the kitchen. “I’ll make crna kafa,” Milica said.

Kristina wrinkled her nose. “Ugh, no,” she said loudly. “That stuff is terrible.”

Milica paused. “I can make tea instead.”

Kristina waved her hand. “Why do you people drink that every day? It’s so bitter. It’s like you enjoy suffering,” she said, walking into the living room.

Milica’s shoulders tightened. “It’s just coffee,” she replied, turning back to the counter.

Kristina continued, “And in those tiny cups, too. It’s not even real coffee. You should switch to something normal.”

Milica placed the džezva back in the cupboard with a small thud. “Okay,” she said, voice flat.

Her father, who had been reading, looked up from the armchair. “Kristina,” he said quietly.

Kristina shrugged and flopped onto the sofa. “What? I’m just saying,” she replied.

Milica brought a glass of water and set it on the table. “Here,” she said.

Kristina glanced at it. “Finally,” she said, then leaned back and scrolled her phone while Milica stood by the doorway for a second before walking back to the kitchen.
